skill.name: find-skills
skill.description: Helps agents search the open skills ecosystem, compare candidate skills, and guide users through safe installation choices.
skill.summary: Finds relevant agent skills and explains how to evaluate and install them.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command patterns and hardcoded URLs, plus weak-cryptography alerts. Review found the weak-cryptography alerts are false positives in prose and examples, while the external command and network findings are legitimate because the skill directs agents to use the Skills CLI and skills.sh. No malicious intent or prompt injection was found, but third-party skill installation with global scope and skipped confirmation warrants a marketplace warning.

medium_findings.title: Global third-party skill installation with automatic confirmation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent that it can install a selected skill with a global install command and the automatic yes flag. This is not malicious by itself, but it can install untrusted third-party content into the user environment if source reputation checks are skipped.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The install command is explicit and semantically relevant to the skill purpose. Confidence is below very high because the document also tells the agent to verify source reputation before recommending a skill.

medium_findings.title: Network-enabled CLI workflow for searching and adding skills
medium_findings.description: The documented workflow uses the Skills CLI and the skills.sh site to discover and install packages. This requires external network access and package manager execution, which creates supply-chain exposure even though the use is expected for this skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static network and command findings match the intended workflow. The risk is contextual supply-chain exposure rather than evidence of credential theft or hidden execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded marketplace URLs are disclosed user-facing links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to skills.sh pages used for browsing and learning more about recommended skills. They are not hidden endpoints and no evidence shows data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links to the skill marketplace. No request body, credential handling, or covert destination appears in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are false positives in prose
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography static findings occur in descriptive text, examples, repository names, and category labels. No cryptographic implementation, hashing call, or security-sensitive algorithm selection is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review confirms these are natural-language or markdown examples rather than code. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar algorithm use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented package manager execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents npx-based commands for finding, adding, checking, updating, and initializing skills. These commands should be run only after user intent and package source are verified.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in the documentation and use a package-manager execution path. The pattern is expected but remains security relevant for publication review.

content.value_statement: Users often do not know which agent skill fits a task. This skill searches the skills ecosystem, compares options, and guides safer installation decisions.
actual_capabilities: Identifies when a user request may be solved by an installable agent skill.
Uses skills.sh and the Skills CLI as discovery sources.
Suggests search terms based on the user domain and task.
Compares candidate skills using install count, source reputation, and repository signals.
Presents recommended skills with installation guidance and learning links.
Explains what to do when no matching skill is found.
limitations: It depends on live marketplace and CLI results that can change over time.
It does not verify the full source code of every third-party skill.
It may miss private, unpublished, or newly released skills.
Installation commands can modify the user environment and require consent.

use_cases.title: Discover a workflow skill
use_cases.description: Find a skill for repeated tasks such as changelogs, reviews, testing, or deployment.

use_cases.title: Compare trusted skill options
use_cases.description: Review install counts, maintainers, and source reputation before choosing a skill.

use_cases.title: Extend an agent setup
use_cases.description: Add relevant capabilities to Claude, Codex, or Claude Code without manually browsing repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Find a basic skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Find a skill that helps with writing changelogs. Show me the best option and why it fits.

prompt_templates.title: Compare options
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for skills for React performance work. Compare the top options by source reputation and install count.

prompt_templates.title: Check installation risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Find a skill for CI debugging, then assess whether I should install it based on maintainer trust and repository signals.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a skill rollout
prompt_templates.prompt: Find skills for documentation, pull request review, and testing. Recommend a safe installation order for a team environment.

output_examples.input: Find a skill for pull request reviews.
output_examples.output: The response lists one or more review-focused skills, explains source trust signals, and asks before installing anything.

output_examples.input: Can you help me make my Next.js app faster?
output_examples.output: The response searches for performance-related skills, summarizes likely matches, and suggests a verified option when available.

output_examples.input: No matching skill exists for my workflow.
output_examples.output: The response says no suitable skill was found, offers direct help, and suggests creating a custom skill for repeated use.

best_practices: Confirm the user wants marketplace search before running CLI commands.
Prefer skills from reputable maintainers with visible adoption signals.
Review source and installation scope before adding a third-party skill.
anti_patterns: Do not install a skill only because it appears in search results.
Do not skip user consent before modifying the environment.
Do not recommend unknown sources without explaining the trust risk.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps find agent skills that match a user task and explains how to evaluate them.

faq.question: Does it install skills automatically?
faq.answer: It can provide installation guidance, but installation should happen only after user approval.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: How does it choose recommendations?
faq.answer: It considers task fit, install count, maintainer reputation, and repository trust signals.

faq.question: Can it review the security of every skill?
faq.answer: No. It gives selection guidance, but each third-party skill should still be reviewed before installation.

faq.question: What happens when no skill is found?
faq.answer: It offers to help with the task directly and may suggest creating a new custom skill.
